evidence: Vrijen volunteered to handle communications. Anna acknowledged this and mentioned she wants to do less of the logistics work this year, asking if Vrijen would take over more of the event coordination responsibilities.
Vrijen agreed absolutely... he's focused on getting his name out there right now

Is there a fundamental mismatch between how we define 'technical' vs. 'power user'? Perhaps the most sophisticated users of any system are those who think in first principles about *how* the system should work, regardless of whether they can build it themselves.

Vrijen described himself as becoming a '1% or 0.1% power user' of products he embeds with—someone who dives so deep into a platform that he reaches functionality most users never discover. Yet he simultaneously identifies as 'non-technical'.

Perhaps frequent relocation doesn't just make you adaptable—it makes you *good at noticing* adaptation. The people who moved often may not be more flexible—they may be more practiced at recognizing when to stop trying to adapt and start trying to belong.

source_display: Human Support Systems vs Human-Centered AI
evidence: Zain's entire thesis centers on what he calls 'human support systems'—infrastructure needed to navigate an AI-automated future... What if the problem isn't that humans need better tools to be more competitive—but that humans have internalized a competitive paradigm that AI has actually already disrupted?

source_display: Small World Algorithm: Trust via Inefficiency
evidence: The moments that actually create trust are often the *unfiltered* overlaps that slip through—the random TikTok connections, the chance hallway conversations, the serendipitous reconnections. We optimize for efficiency, but trust is created by inefficiency.

source_display: Small World Algorithm: Trust via Inefficiency
evidence: The Logan connection—Zain randomly connected with Vrijen's co-founder on TikTok, and that morning during their check-in, both mentioned this 'interesting person they were talking to'—creates a 'glitch in the matrix' moment. Both participants treated this as meaningful.

Vrijen and Zain spent several minutes discussing dual monitor setups—Vrijen calling it a 'complete luxury' and 'indulgence'... Maybe all productivity is just identity performance. The monitor isn't about doing more—it's about being the *kind of person* who cares about the details.

Authentic content creates trust slowly and transactionally, whereas clickbait extracts attention quickly and parasitically. The people with real things to say don't need to rely on cheap tactics not because they're morally superior, but because they're playing a different game entirely—one where the asset is long-term relationship.
Both Vrijen and Zain strongly emphasized belief in authentic content and substantive contribution over 'cheap tactics,' clickbait, or rage-bait.
Vrijen will provide source code access to his Zo-built systems... Vrijen will introduce Zain to FOHE leadership

Zain had randomly connected with Logan (Vrijen's co-founder) on TikTok after seeing her content about work and skills. Neither realized the shared connection until Vrijen mentioned it during their call.

Careerspan isn't a replacement for Handshake or any recruiting platform—it's the 'missing layer' that makes their products defensible. This reframes the entire partnership narrative from competition to symbiosis.

Handshake has 1,600 universities but no way to capture behavioral signal at scale. Careerspan has the coaching infrastructure to capture signal but lacks distribution. The combination creates a data moat neither could build alone.
Logan takes ownership of action items... refines messaging and proof points. Collaborative - works closely with Vrijen on strategy.

The entire Careerspan thesis rests on the premise that behavioral signal (how people think) is more valuable than profile data (what they've done). This challenges the fundamental premise of LinkedIn and traditional resumes.
The real value isn't in helping someone get one job—it's in tracking their entire career trajectory and predicting future success. This creates a dataset that compounds in value over time.

Positioning Careerspan as a strategic hedge for Handshake's declining core business ($200M, only 6% growth). This acknowledges reality (Handshake is moving away from recruiting) while offering value (we can rejuvenate that line with signal technology).

Emphasizing the 3-person team as an asset (low acquisition overhead) turns a potential weakness into strength. In an era of bloated startups with high burn rates, being lean makes you an attractive acquisition target.
Extended conversation about potential partnership with Handshake... Careerspan could become the infrastructure layer for the entire university recruiting ecosystem
Vrijen and Logan reviewed various deal pipeline contacts... Both have equal voice in refining narratives and proof points.
